text.title: Business Analysis Planning: Six Steps and Approach Selection
text.summary: Six-step BA planning procedure, approach-selection decision rules, elicitation technique table, and deliverable types.

rule.statement: Before selecting an approach, answer four questions: what is the initiative scope, what type of change (new system/process/strategy), what is the organizational culture (formal vs. informal), and what constraints exist (time, budget, resources).
rule.rationale: Approach selection without context produces plan-driven defaults for agile contexts and change-driven defaults for regulated projects — both are wrong. Context drives approach, not project phase or team preference.

rule.statement: Apply this decision rule: if requirements clarity is high AND change frequency is low, select Plan-Driven; if clarity is low OR continuous discovery is active, select Change-Driven; otherwise select Hybrid and declare per-artifact which is baselined vs. living.
rule.rationale: Plan-Driven vs. Change-Driven is not a team culture preference — it is a function of requirements uncertainty. The Hybrid case requires explicit artifact classification to prevent silent drift toward one extreme.

list.item: Plan-Driven — upfront baseline, sequential phases, extensive documentation; for stable regulatory or fixed-scope initiatives.
Change-Driven — iterative, adaptive, just-enough documentation; for continuous discovery or evolving requirements.
Hybrid — combination; declare each artifact as baselined (e.g., BRD v1.0) or living (e.g., stakeholder list) at plan time.

rule.statement: Always include a hidden-stakeholders review step covering: legal, infosec, procurement, works council/union, accessibility, downstream API consumers. The most common BA planning failure is missing a stakeholder discovered during elicitation.
rule.rationale: Hidden stakeholders discovered after elicitation begins trigger scope changes that invalidate the plan; finding them at planning time costs a single conversation vs. re-elicitation, re-documentation, and re-validation.

list.item: Sponsors — executives providing resources and authority.
Users — direct solution users (employees, customers, partners).
Implementers — team building the solution (developers, architects).
Regulators — compliance bodies with mandatory requirements.
Support — operations, help desk, maintenance teams.

rule.statement: Track actual vs. planned elicitation effort in a CSV alongside the plan and recalibrate every two weeks; the first estimate is always wrong because "1 workshop" hides 4 hours of prep, 2 hours of facilitation, and 6 hours of synthesis.
rule.rationale: Elicitation effort is the most consistently underestimated BA activity; without actuals tracking, every plan iteration repeats the same underestimate and projects run over.

list.item: Interviews — for deep understanding of individual stakeholder needs.
Workshops — for consensus building across multiple stakeholders in one session.
Surveys — for broad input from many stakeholders when scheduling is infeasible.
Observation — for process understanding when stated behavior differs from actual behavior.
Document Analysis — for understanding existing state from existing artifacts.

rule.statement: Tie every BA activity to a deliverable ID (DEL-03) and every deliverable to a requirement set; this enables traceability querying and makes the plan auditable.
rule.rationale: A plan with activities but no deliverable IDs cannot answer "what did the BA produce and when?" — the question every programme audit asks first.

section.title: Step 6: Establish Governance

rule.statement: Name a single human approver in the BA Approach Document frontmatter (approver: name@domain) and enforce via CODEOWNERS; without a named approver the plan is unverifiable and unauditable.
rule.rationale: BABOK v3 ch. 3 treats governance as mandatory, not optional; a plan without decision authority is a suggestion, and suggestions have no binding power over scope, timeline, or deliverable quality.

list.item: Who approves requirements — named role, not generic "leadership".
How changes are handled — change request process with an owner.
Review and sign-off cadence — specific schedule, not "as needed".
Escalation path — defined chain when approval is blocked.

section.reference: BA Approach Document with YAML frontmatter for all governance fields

antipattern.description: Governance section contains vague text ("approval by leadership") instead of named roles.
antipattern.reason: No named approver means no accountability; auditors and stakeholders cannot determine who has authority, and the BA cannot obtain binding sign-off.
